items.title: Diagnose variance before changing the model
items.avoid: Adding regularization or reducing capacity when both training and validation performance are poor.
items.prefer: Compare train vs validation metrics and plot learning curves; treat large train–val gaps as variance, dual poor scores as bias.
items.why: Variance fixes (simpler model, more regularization) worsen underfitting when bias dominates.
items.example: If train accuracy is 99% and val is 72%, try max_depth=5 before switching to a linear model with 60% train accuracy.

items.title: Use more data when learning curves show a gap
items.avoid: Stacking more model capacity when validation error stays high despite growing training set size.
items.prefer: Collect more labeled data or use data augmentation when learning curves show validation improving with n but still below training.
items.why: Additional data primarily reduces variance without necessarily increasing bias.
items.example: Doubling training rows moved val accuracy from 78% to 85% while train stayed near 95%—variance was the bottleneck.

items.title: Report stability, not just a single score
items.avoid: Publishing one hold-out accuracy without fold std or confidence interval.
items.prefer: Report cross-validation mean ± std or bootstrap CI so stakeholders see variance of the estimate and model stability.
items.why: A lucky split hides high-variance models that fail on the next retrain.
items.example: Report 'CV accuracy 84% ± 4%' instead of 'test accuracy 88%' from one cherry-picked split.
